content:
Middle East escalation triggers energy shock and a global “risk-off” rotation

Event Synopsis

  • Escalation in the US–Israel conflict with Iran raised immediate concerns about disruptions to energy supply and shipping through the Strait of Hormuz; market pricing shifted toward a higher near-term risk premium in crude and European gas. (Source: Reuters)
  • Brent briefly traded above the low-$80s per barrel in early moves after the escalation, reinforcing renewed inflation concerns and raising the probability of policy caution by major central banks. (Source: Reuters)

Cross-Asset Market Reaction

  • Rates: US 10Y around 3.97%; Indonesia 10Y around 6.43%—both biased higher on inflation risk, with moves dominated by energy headlines rather than growth data. (Source: BRIDS)
  • FX: DXY firmer around 100; USD/IDR around 16,800, with EM FX generally softer versus the USD on higher oil and reduced risk appetite. (Source: Reuters)
  • Commodities / risk assets: Brent around $78–$82 and WTI around $72; gold higher around $5,346/oz; Asian and European equities broadly weaker, while energy/defense outperformed. (Source: Reuters)

Policy Relevance

  • Inflation: Higher oil and gas prices raise headline inflation risks quickly, and can slow the pace of disinflation even if core demand is cooling.
  • Stability / flows: Risk-off conditions tend to tighten financial conditions (USD strength, wider credit premia) and increase pressure on oil-importing EMs via FX and imported inflation.

Forward Watch Points

  • Monitor shipping/insurance conditions and any coordinated strategic stock or supply responses that could cap energy price spikes. (Source: Reuters)
  • Watch for second-round effects: fuel pass-through into transport/food prices and any shift in market-implied central bank paths.

Indonesia: rupiah and equities weaken as global risk appetite falls; bond market focus on funding and stability

Event Synopsis

  • Indonesian assets came under pressure amid the global risk-off impulse: equities sold off and the rupiah remained under depreciation pressure versus the USD. (Source: Investing.com)
  • Local fixed income attention remained on government bond yield volatility and liquidity conditions as investors reassess inflation risks from higher energy prices and potential global rate repricing. (Source: BRIDS)

Cross-Asset Market Reaction

  • Rates: US 10Y around 3.97% (little changed vs recent levels); Indonesia 10Y around 6.43% (slightly higher in recent sessions). (Source: BRIDS)
  • Equities: Indonesia’s IDX Composite around 8,036 (down on the session shown), consistent with regional EM underperformance during oil-driven risk aversion. (Source: Investing.com)
  • FX: DXY around 100; USD/IDR around 16,800—pressure consistent with higher oil import costs and reduced appetite for EM carry. (Source: Reuters)

Policy Relevance

  • Exchange rate stability: A stronger USD and higher oil prices can tighten the policy trade-off—supporting IDR stability may require maintaining a restrictive stance longer than otherwise.
  • Fiscal/financing: Higher global risk premia can lift the marginal cost of funding; maintaining market confidence hinges on clear coordination between debt issuance strategy, liquidity management, and inflation credibility.

Forward Watch Points

  • Track USD/IDR sensitivity to oil and global rates; watch for signs of disorderly moves (thin liquidity, one-way positioning). (Source: BI)
  • Monitor domestic bond auction outcomes (bid-cover, non-resident participation) and any shift in banking system demand for SBN/SRBI.

Europe: energy-driven inflation risk pushes yields up and complicates near-term easing expectations

Event Synopsis

  • European rates repriced higher as investors reassessed near-term inflation risks from the energy shock; German 10Y yields moved up toward the mid-2.6% area. (Source: Trading Economics)
  • Markets shifted to a more cautious view on the timing/pace of rate cuts as the energy impulse threatens to re-accelerate headline inflation even if growth is soft. (Source: Reuters)

Cross-Asset Market Reaction

  • Rates: US 10Y around 3.97%; Indonesia 10Y around 6.43%; core Europe yields higher led by bunds as inflation breakevens rose with energy. (Source: Trading Economics)
  • Equities: European equities fell, with travel and consumer-facing sectors underperforming; energy and defense held up better. (Source: Reuters)
  • FX / commodities: DXY around 100; EUR/USD softer as Europe’s energy vulnerability increased; Brent and European gas up sharply; gold bid. (Source: Reuters)

Policy Relevance

  • Inflation vs growth: Europe faces a difficult mix—higher imported energy inflation alongside a potential hit to real incomes and confidence.
  • Financial stability: Higher yields and higher energy costs can worsen credit conditions for leveraged sectors; watch for stress in rate-sensitive assets and pockets of high energy dependence.

Forward Watch Points

  • Monitor near-term inflation releases and market-based inflation expectations for signs the energy shock is becoming more persistent. (Source: ECB)
  • Watch European gas storage and LNG flows as a key determinant of the inflation path into the next seasonal restocking cycle.

US: equities prove resilient despite higher oil; bond market trades inflation risk rather than growth shock

Event Synopsis

  • US equities were volatile but avoided a broad capitulation as investors balanced higher oil (inflation risk) against a still-uncertain impact on growth and corporate earnings. (Source: AP)
  • Treasury yields tilted upward on the day as the market emphasized near-term inflation impulse from energy rather than an immediate recession signal. (Source: WSJ)

Cross-Asset Market Reaction

  • Rates: US 10Y around 3.97%; Indonesia 10Y around 6.43%—both reflecting a global inflation-risk impulse from energy rather than local data surprises. (Source: BRIDS)
  • Equities: S&P 500 roughly flat near 6,882; Dow slightly lower near 48,905; Nasdaq higher near 22,749—energy/defense up, travel weaker. (Source: AP)
  • FX / commodities: DXY around 100; USD/JPY around the mid-150s; USD/CNH steadier; Brent around $78–$82, WTI around $72, gold higher around $5,346/oz. (Source: Reuters)

Policy Relevance

  • Inflation outlook: Energy-driven headline inflation risk may slow the pace of easing even if core inflation trends remain favorable.
  • Market stability: The key near-term risk is a feedback loop from higher energy into inflation expectations and rates volatility, tightening conditions more than the real-economy shock warrants.

Forward Watch Points

  • Monitor near-term inflation expectations, fuel price pass-through, and whether financial conditions tighten materially (credit spreads, liquidity, volatility). (Source: Reuters)
  • Watch for any coordinated

Middle East escalation drives an energy shock and a global risk-off move

Event Synopsis

  • Military escalation around Iran raised immediate concern over disruption to oil and LNG flows, with markets repricing energy supply risk and shipping risk as a near-term macro shock. (Source: The Guardian)
  • Oil prices jumped sharply in early trading as investors focused on potential constraints through key regional routes and facilities. (Source: Reuters)

Cross-Asset Market Reaction

  • Rates: US 10Y ~3.98% (little changed); Indonesia 10Y ~6.43% (little changed). (Source: Trading Economics)
  • FX: DXY ~97.81 (firmer); USD/IDR ~16,640 (IDR weaker); EUR/USD ~1.18 area (softer tone); USD/JPY ~156 (little changed); USD/CNH ~6.86 (CNH softer). (Source: Trading Economics)
  • Commodities: Brent ~US$80/bbl (higher); WTI ~US$73/bbl (higher); Gold higher on safe-haven demand. (Source: Reuters)

Policy Relevance

  • This is an adverse “inflation plus risk” shock: higher energy costs can lift headline inflation while simultaneously tightening financial conditions via weaker risk sentiment. (Source: Reuters)
  • For EMs, the main transmission is through the USD channel and energy-import bills: more FX pressure, higher hedging demand, and greater sensitivity to capital outflows if volatility persists. (Source: Reuters)

Forward Watch Points

  • Monitor physical disruptions (shipping constraints, insurance premia, LNG outages) and whether the oil move persists beyond the first risk repricing. (Source: Reuters)
  • Watch for any coordinated policy response signals (SPR guidance, shipping advisories, or de-escalation messaging) that could cap tail risks. (Source: Reuters)

Indonesia: Rupiah stress intensifies; BI signals active stabilization posture

Event Synopsis

  • The rupiah weakened to around IDR 16,640 per USD, its weakest level since the late-1990s crisis period, with markets citing fiscal concerns and broader USD strength/risk aversion. (Source: Reuters)
  • Authorities indicated readiness to intervene to maintain orderly FX conditions, aiming to limit overshooting and preserve market functioning. (Source: Reuters)

Cross-Asset Market Reaction

  • Rates: US 10Y ~3.98% (little changed); Indonesia 10Y ~6.43% (little changed, but sensitive to FX stress). (Source: Pasardana.id)
  • FX: DXY ~97.81 (firmer); USD/IDR ~16,640 (IDR weaker). (Source: Reuters)
  • Broader risk: EM risk sentiment deteriorated alongside higher oil and geopolitical risk, raising the probability of more defensive positioning toward high-beta EM assets. (Source: Reuters)

Policy Relevance

  • FX weakness raises near-term inflation risk via pass-through (fuel, imported goods) and can tighten domestic financial conditions even without policy-rate changes. (Source: BI)
  • Market focus is on policy credibility and fiscal guardrails: sustained rupiah pressure can increase funding costs, reduce foreign demand for local bonds, and narrow monetary policy room. (Source: Reuters)

Forward Watch Points

  • Track BI’s daily rupiah-and-bond stability indicators and evidence of two-way liquidity returning in FX and SBN markets. (Source: BI)
  • Watch offshore NDF pricing and local bond auction outcomes for signs of stress transmission into rates and funding. (Source: Reuters)

China: Authorities lean against yuan strength; signals tighter management of FX conditions

Event Synopsis

  • China’s central bank moved to slow the yuan’s prior rally by lowering the FX risk reserve ratio on forward sales, reducing banks’ cost of providing USD hedges and signaling a preference to curb one-way appreciation. (Source: Caixin)
  • Market interpretation: policymakers are seeking to stabilize expectations and preserve export competitiveness while keeping overall financial conditions supportive. (Source: Reuters)

Cross-Asset Market Reaction

  • Rates: US 10Y ~3.98% (little changed); Indonesia 10Y ~6.43% (little changed). (Source: Trading Economics)
  • FX: DXY ~97.81 (firmer); USD/CNH ~6.86 (CNH slightly weaker); USD/IDR ~16,640 (IDR weaker). (Source: Reuters)
  • Risk linkage: In a risk-off tape, CNH sensitivity to USD moves can amplify broader Asia FX weakness, especially for deficit/energy-importing markets. (Source: Reuters)

Policy Relevance

  • FX management is being used as a macro-stability tool: limiting sharp currency swings helps contain imported inflation volatility and reduces destabilizing capital-flow dynamics. (Source: Reuters)
  • For the region, a steadier CNH reduces the probability of competitive depreciation dynamics, but it does not remove USD-driven pressure when global risk aversion rises. (Source: Reuters)

Forward Watch Points

  • Watch daily fixing behavior and onshore/offshore spread dynamics for signs of stronger administrative smoothing or renewed one-way pressure. (Source: Reuters)
  • Monitor whether corporates step up USD demand (hedging/imports) as volatility rises—this is often the near-term driver of CNH moves. (Source: Reuters)

Europe: Energy-linked inflation fear hits risk appetite, with UK/European equities under pressure

Event Synopsis

  • European risk assets came under pressure as the energy shock raised concern about a renewed rise in headline inflation while growth remains fragile—an unfavorable mix for policy and earnings. (Source: Reuters)
  • Equity declines were broad-based, with energy winners unable to offset losses in cyclicals and rate-sensitive sectors as investors repriced macro uncertainty. (Source: Reuters)

Cross-Asset Market Reaction

  • Rates: US 10Y ~3.98% (little changed); Indonesia 10Y ~6.43% (little changed); core European yields were steadier than equities, consistent with a growth-risk bid. (Source: MarketScreener)
  • Equities: STOXX Europe 600 down (~1.5% intraday); DAX down (~2.2%); FTSE 100 down (~0.7%). (Source: MarketScreener)
  • FX/Commodities: DXY ~97.81 (firmer); EUR/USD ~1.18 area (softer); Brent ~US$80 and WTI ~US$73 (higher), reinforcing inflation concerns; Gold firmer. (Source: MarketScreener)

Policy Relevance

  • Higher energy prices can slow disinflation and complicate near-term rate decisions; policymakers must weigh inflation persistence against weaker demand and tighter financial conditions. (Source: Reuters)
  • For fiscal authorities, renewed energy-price volatility raises pressure for targeted support while preserving credibility—especially where debt dynamics are already sensitive to rates. (Source: Reuters)

Forward Watch Points

  • Track European gas and power benchmarks and whether the initial spike persists; sustained moves would raise the probability

Middle East escalation drives oil shock; risk assets de-rate on supply disruption risk

Event Synopsis

  • Oil prices surged after the U.S. and Israel struck Iran and attacks disrupted shipping near/through the Strait of Hormuz, raising the risk of a near-term supply squeeze in a critical global chokepoint. (Source: Reuters)
  • Market focus shifted from growth to inflation risk via energy, with spillovers to EM risk premia and global transportation/shipping costs.

Cross-Asset Market Reaction

  • Government bonds: US 10Y 4.2355% (up ~4 bps); Indonesia 10Y 7.202% (ticks higher). (Source: Reuters)
  • FX: DXY ~98.18; USD/IDR ~16,355 (little changed); EUR/USD ~1.1653; USD/JPY ~147.23; USD/CNH ~7.1836. (Source: Reuters)
  • Commodities: Brent ~$66.61; WTI directionally higher on the shock; gold ~$3,370/oz (profit-taking vs prior spike). (Source: Reuters)

Policy Relevance

  • Inflation: A sustained oil/shipping shock is a direct upside risk to near-term headline inflation globally and would slow disinflation even if demand weakens.
  • Stability/capital flows: Higher oil widens external financing needs for oil importers and can amplify risk-off outflows from EM local-currency bonds and FX, tightening domestic financial conditions.

Forward Watch Points

  • Watch: any confirmation of prolonged disruption (insurance premia, tanker routes, port delays) and producer response (OPEC+ signals, strategic stock draw discussions).
  • Track: second-round effects—freight rates, airline/transport pricing, and inflation expectations that could influence central-bank reaction functions.

U.S. rates reprice on weak demand at Treasury auction; yields back up despite softer-dollar tone

Event Synopsis

  • Longer-dated U.S. Treasury yields rose after a weak 10-year auction, interrupting a multi-session decline and tightening global financial conditions at the margin. (Source: Reuters)
  • Attention is also on imminent Fed Board personnel decisions, which markets see as potentially influential for the medium-term policy path and risk premia. (Source: Reuters)

Cross-Asset Market Reaction

  • Government bonds: US 10Y ~4.224–4.236% (up); Indonesia 10Y ~7.202% (higher). (Source: Reuters)
  • Equities: S&P 500 ~6,345 (+0.7%); Nasdaq ~21,163 (+~1.2%); Europe STOXX 600 slightly lower; Japan Nikkei ~40,795 (+0.6%). (Source: Reuters)
  • FX & commodities snapshot: DXY ~98.18; USD/IDR ~16,355; EUR/USD ~1.1653; USD/JPY ~147.23; Brent ~$66.61; gold ~$3,370. (Source: Reuters)

Policy Relevance

  • Monetary policy space: Higher term premia (auction-driven) can tighten conditions without a policy-rate move, complicating calibration for central banks trying to balance growth risks against inflation risks.
  • Market stability: If auction weakness persists, it can transmit quickly into mortgage/corporate funding costs and raise volatility, especially alongside geopolitical shocks.

Forward Watch Points

  • Monitor: upcoming U.S. supply/auction outcomes and any shift in bid-to-cover/indirect bidder participation as a real-time gauge of duration demand.
  • Watch: communication and appointments related to the Fed that could change perceived reaction function and the pricing of rate cuts/hikes.

Indonesia: rupiah steadies near 16,355 as global risk-off keeps local yields pressured

Event Synopsis

  • Indonesia’s FX and rates remain sensitive to global risk sentiment: USD/IDR held around 16,355 while the Indonesia 10Y yield was indicated higher around 7.202%, reflecting a higher risk premium backdrop. (Source: Reuters)
  • External shocks (oil price moves and global rate volatility) are dominating near-term price action more than domestic micro news, keeping local markets in a defensive posture.

Cross-Asset Market Reaction

  • Government bonds: US 10Y ~4.2355%; Indonesia 10Y ~7.202% (higher). (Source: Reuters)
  • FX: DXY ~98.18; USD/IDR ~16,355 (little changed); EUR/USD ~1.1653; USD/JPY ~147.23; USD/CNH ~7.1836. (Source: Reuters)
  • Commodities relevant for Indonesia: Brent ~$66.61 (down on the day in the snapshot but elevated sensitivity to Middle East headlines); gold ~$3,370/oz. (Source: Reuters)

Policy Relevance

  • Inflation and FX stability: Higher global oil risk raises imported inflation risk and can pressure the current account and rupiah via a weaker trade balance and wider risk premia.
  • Capital flows/financial stability: Higher local yields can support carry at the margin, but risk-off episodes typically dominate—raising the importance of credible FX/liquidity backstops and clear policy signaling.

Forward Watch Points

  • Watch: USD/IDR liquidity conditions (onshore/offshore basis), foreign flows into government bonds, and whether local yields decouple from U.S. rates.
  • Monitor: domestic fuel pricing/fiscal buffers if oil volatility persists, and any central bank actions to smooth FX volatility.

Asia risk assets weaken; safe-havens (gold, JPY, CHF) outperform as investors reprice geopolitical tail risk

Event Synopsis

  • Asian markets reflected a broad risk-off tone as investors shifted toward safety following Middle East escalation, with equities softer and defensives outperforming cyclicals. (Source: Reuters)
  • Risk pricing centered on higher energy and shipping uncertainty, with EM Asia particularly exposed through imported inflation and portfolio flow sensitivity.

Cross-Asset Market Reaction

  • Government bonds: US 10Y ~4.2355% (higher); Indonesia 10Y ~7.202% (higher). (Source: Reuters)
  • Equities: regional performance mixed-to-weaker in risk-off trade; global backdrop still supported by prior U.S. tech-led gains. (Source: Reuters)
  • FX & commodities: DXY ~98.18; USD/IDR ~16,355; EUR/USD ~1.1653; USD/JPY ~147.23 (JPY firmer in risk-off); Brent ~$66.61; gold ~$3,370/oz. (Source: Reuters)

Policy Relevance

  • Growth vs inflation: A geopolitical risk premium tends to tighten financial conditions and weaken confidence (growth-negative) while higher energy costs are inflation-positive—creating a difficult trade-off for central banks.
  • Capital flows and stability: Risk-off periods can produce abrupt portfolio outflows and FX pressure in EMs; this increases the value of credible reserves management, swap lines where available, and clear communication on liquidity provision.

Forward Watch Points

  • Watch: whether safe-haven demand becomes persistent (JPY strength, gold bid) versus a short-lived headline reaction; persistence would imply tighter global conditions.
  • Monitor: volatility indicators and cross-currency funding stress (especially USD funding) that could transmit from geopolitics into broader financial stability risks.

Middle East escalation drives an oil shock and broad risk-off positioning

Event Synopsis

  • Oil prices surged after U.S. and Israeli strikes on Iran over the weekend, raising fears of major disruption to energy flows through the Strait of Hormuz (a critical chokepoint for global oil and LNG shipments). (Source: Financial Times)
  • Market pricing shifted quickly toward higher near-term energy inflation risk and weaker global growth, as investors moved into traditional safe havens. (Source: AP News)

Cross-Asset Market Reaction

  • Government bonds: U.S. 10Y fell to ~3.93% (safe-haven bid); Indonesia 10Y ~6.43% (up ~1bp in the latest session). (Source: Trading Economics)
  • Equities: U.S. equity futures down ~1.4%; major Asian equities broadly lower (notably Japan and Hong Kong). (Source: AP News)
  • FX & commodities: DXY firmer on haven demand; USD/IDR little changed around ~16,828; Brent up ~8% near ~$79; WTI up ~7.5% near ~$72; gold up sharply (safe-haven bid). (Source: AP News)

Policy Relevance

  • Inflation: A sustained oil shock would raise headline inflation and inflation expectations quickly, even if underlying demand weakens—complicating near-term easing plans in multiple jurisdictions. (Source: Financial Times)
  • Stability and capital flows: Risk-off conditions can tighten global financial conditions, pressure EMFX, and increase liquidity demand; policymakers should prepare for higher volatility in funding markets and FX hedging costs. (Source: AP News)

Forward Watch Points

  • Watch evidence of physical supply disruption (shipping/insurance availability, tanker traffic, and any confirmed damage to energy infrastructure) as the key determinant of whether the oil move persists. (Source: Financial Times)
  • Monitor whether the shock transmits into broader inflation expectations (breakevens, consumer inflation expectations) and credit spreads, which would signal rising financial-stability risk. (Source: AP News)

Indonesia: Rupiah bond yields edge higher as policymakers brace for volatility

Event Synopsis

  • Indonesia’s rupiah government bond market weakened modestly in the latest session, with benchmark 10Y yields around ~6.40%–6.43% (about +1bp), alongside steady secondary-market turnover. (Source: Pasardana/BNI Sekuritas citing PHEI & Bloomberg)
  • The tone of local market commentary emphasizes heightened sensitivity to global risk moves (oil shock and safe-haven flows), raising the probability of short-term volatility in SBN pricing and yields. (Source: Pasardana/BNI Sekuritas)

Cross-Asset Market Reaction

  • Government bonds: U.S. 10Y ~3.93%; Indonesia 10Y ~6.43% (up ~1bp). (Source: Trading Economics)
  • Equities: Global risk sentiment weaker on the oil shock, which typically weighs on high-beta EM assets even when domestic fundamentals are unchanged. (Source: AP News)
  • FX & commodities: DXY firmer; USD/IDR little changed around ~16,828; higher oil prices are a negative terms-of-trade impulse for net oil importers and can widen external financing needs at the margin. (Source: AP News)

Policy Relevance

  • Inflation and growth: Higher crude prices, if sustained, can raise Indonesia’s headline inflation while squeezing real incomes—tightening the trade-off between supporting growth and protecting price stability. (Source: Financial Times)
  • Capital flows and FX stability: In global risk-off episodes, maintaining orderly conditions in the bond market and ensuring adequate FX liquidity become more important than signaling shifts in the policy rate path. (Source: Pasardana/BNI Sekuritas)

Forward Watch Points

  • Track non-resident flows in SBN and daily USD/IDR liquidity conditions; stress typically shows up first in bid-ask spreads and FX swap pricing rather than the spot rate. (Source: Pasardana/BNI Sekuritas)
  • Monitor domestic fuel pricing and any fiscal mitigation measures, which determine how quickly global oil prices pass through to CPI and inflation expectations. (Source: Financial Times)

China: PBoC move to curb yuan strength lifts USD/CNH and signals preference for two-way FX risk

Event Synopsis

  • The PBoC cut the FX risk reserve ratio on forward FX sales from 20% to 0%, effective March 2, reducing the cost of USD buying via forwards and leaning against rapid yuan appreciation. (Source: Caixin)
  • USD/CNH jumped to around 6.86 shortly after the announcement (about +0.3% on the move), consistent with official preference for less one-way RMB strength. (Source: investingLive)

Cross-Asset Market Reaction

  • Government bonds: U.S. 10Y ~3.93%; Indonesia 10Y ~6.43% (up ~1bp). (Source: Trading Economics)
  • FX: USD/CNH moved higher after the change; broader USD support is also coming from geopolitical risk (haven demand). DXY firmer; USD/IDR little changed around ~16,828. (Source: AP News)
  • Commodities: Oil and gold remain the dominant macro transmission channels today; higher energy prices can complicate China’s growth-inflation mix even if FX is stabilized. (Source: Financial Times)

Policy Relevance

  • Exchange-rate stability: The reserve-ratio change is a targeted signal—authorities want to avoid an overly strong RMB that tightens financial conditions for exporters, while discouraging one-way expectations that can destabilize positioning. (Source: Caixin)
  • Capital flows: Cheaper hedging via forwards can reduce pressure from corporates needing USD cover, potentially smoothing cross-border flows without deploying more visible spot intervention. (Source: investingLive)

Forward Watch Points

  • Watch daily fixings versus market estimates and the onshore-offshore spread (CNY-CNH) for signs of further policy leaning or rising stress. (Source: investingLive)
  • Monitor whether global risk-off dynamics overpower China’s FX signaling—particularly if oil-driven inflation risk lifts the USD broadly and tightens EM financial conditions. (Source: AP News)

US rates fall on safe-haven demand even as inflation risks rise via energy

Event Synopsis

  • U.S. Treasuries rallied as investors sought safety, pushing the U.S. 10Y yield down to ~3.93% (a notable move lower as geopolitical risk dominated positioning). (Source: Trading Economics)
  • At the same time, the oil spike is mechanically inflationary near term, creating a policy tension: weaker risk appetite and growth concerns versus higher headline inflation risk. (Source: Financial Times)

Cross-Asset Market Reaction

  • Government bonds: U.S. 10Y ~3.93%; Indonesia 10Y ~6.43%. (Source: Trading Economics)
  • Equities: U.S. futures down ~1.4% with broad declines across Asia, consistent with risk reduction and higher input-cost fears. (Source: AP News)
  • FX & commodities: DXY firmer; EUR/USD and risk FX softer in a haven-dollar move; USD/JPY pressured by risk-off dynamics; Brent near ~$79 and WTI near ~$72; gold sharply higher. USD/IDR ~16,828, little changed. (Source: AP News)

Policy Relevance

  • Inflation outlook: If oil remains elevated, headline CPI pressures could re-accelerate and spill into expectations, constraining near-term easing even if growth slows. (Source: Financial Times)
  • Financial stability: Falling yields alongside falling equities signals “risk-off” rather than a clean disinflation trade; policymakers should watch liquidity, credit spreads, and funding markets for stress propagation. (Source: AP News)

Forward Watch Points

  • Track whether the oil shock persists beyond the initial gap (physical flow data, shipping conditions), because that will determine whether inflation

Middle East escalation re-prices energy and global risk appetite

Event Synopsis

  • Over the last 24 hours, U.S.–Israeli strikes on Iran intensified market focus on potential oil-supply disruption risks, particularly through the Strait of Hormuz (a critical global transit route). (Source: The Wall Street Journal)
  • Markets are treating this as an inflation-risk shock rather than a growth-positive impulse, given the risk of higher fuel and shipping costs feeding into consumer prices. (Source: AP News)

Cross-Asset Market Reaction

  • Government bonds: U.S. 10Y yield ~4.00% (little changed); Indonesia 10Y ~6.40% (little changed). (Source: Bank Indonesia)
  • FX: DXY ~97.79 (little changed); USD/IDR ~16,750 (little changed). EUR/USD and USD/JPY were not reliably observable from primary sources within the last 24 hours in the material reviewed. (Source: Bank Indonesia)
  • Commodities: Brent around the low-$70s area (risk premium elevated); gold up on safe-haven demand. (Source: The Wall Street Journal)

Policy Relevance

  • Inflation outlook: Upside risk rises via energy, freight, and insurance costs; second-round effects are the key uncertainty (wage/price behavior and pass-through). (Source: AP News)
  • Financial stability and policy space: A sustained oil shock would tighten financial conditions and can reduce room for rate cuts globally, especially for oil-importing EMs exposed to FX depreciation. (Source: The Wall Street Journal)

Forward Watch Points

  • Monitor tangible signs of physical disruption (shipping delays, insurance spikes, tanker diversions) rather than headlines alone; these will determine whether the shock persists. (Source: The Wall Street Journal)
  • Watch near-term inflation expectations and breakevens; a sharp rise would force more restrictive policy signaling even if growth slows. (Source: AP News)

Indonesia: rupiah stability indicators show easier global rates backdrop, but external-risk premium remains the swing factor

Event Synopsis

  • Bank Indonesia’s latest stability update (dated Feb 27, 2026) showed: rupiah at ~16,750 per USD (bid), Indonesia 10Y SBN yield down to ~6.40–6.41%, DXY ~97.79, and U.S. 10Y ~4.004%. (Source: Bank Indonesia)
  • Separately, Indonesia executed sizeable offshore issuance (Dim Sum and euro bonds), reinforcing funding access but increasing market sensitivity to global risk sentiment and term premia. (Source: IFR)

Cross-Asset Market Reaction

  • Government bonds: U.S. 10Y ~4.00% (little changed); Indonesia 10Y ~6.40% (down modestly vs prior close). (Source: Bank Indonesia)
  • FX: DXY ~97.79 (little changed); USD/IDR ~16,750 (steady). (Source: Bank Indonesia)
  • Commodities: Brent in the low-$70s risk zone matters for Indonesia mainly through imported inflation and current-account sensitivity; gold firmer supports risk-off hedging demand. (Source: AP News)

Policy Relevance

  • Exchange-rate stability and capital flows: Stable USD/IDR alongside lower SBN yields suggests near-term conditions are manageable, but external shocks (oil/geopolitics) can rapidly widen Indonesia’s risk premium. (Source: Bank Indonesia)
  • Fiscal/monetary policy space: Offshore issuance confirms market access, but higher global volatility raises refinancing costs and increases the value of policy credibility (inflation anchor, FX management, and coordination). (Source: IFR)

Forward Watch Points

  • Track non-resident flows in SBN and equities and any evidence of hedging stress; these are early-warning signals for rupiah pressure during geopolitical risk spikes. (Source: Bank Indonesia)
  • Monitor how new external borrowing prices relative to peers; widening new-issue concessions would signal tightening external financing conditions. (Source: IFR)

U.S. rates: softer dollar tone as Treasury yields sit near recent lows

Event Synopsis

  • Over the past 24 hours of market commentary, the dollar was described as slipping alongside falling U.S. 10-year yields, reflecting a “lower yields, softer dollar” impulse even as some U.S. data points were firm. (Source: Nasdaq/Barchart)
  • This mix keeps markets sensitive to the balance between “sticky inflation” signals and risk-off flows into duration. (Source: Nasdaq/Barchart)

Cross-Asset Market Reaction

  • Government bonds: U.S. 10Y ~4.00% (near recent lows); Indonesia 10Y ~6.40% (little changed). (Source: Bank Indonesia)
  • FX: DXY ~97.79 (softer bias); USD/IDR ~16,750 (little changed). EUR/USD and USD/CNH were not reliably observable from primary sources within the last 24 hours in the material reviewed; USD/JPY remained near mid-150s in broader coverage but not confirmed by a primary-market feed here. (Source: Bank Indonesia)
  • Commodities: Gold firmer (safe-haven plus softer-dollar impulse); oil dominated by geopolitical premium. (Source: AP News)

Policy Relevance

  • Inflation and growth: Lower long yields typically ease financial conditions, but if the driver is risk-off (not confidence), it can coincide with weaker growth expectations and tighter private credit. (Source: Nasdaq/Barchart)
  • Spillovers to EMs: A softer DXY reduces immediate FX stress, but any renewed oil-driven inflation shock could reverse the move quickly and re-tighten global dollar liquidity. (Source: AP News)

Forward Watch Points

  • Watch whether yields fall because inflation expectations decline (benign) or because growth expectations deteriorate (risk-off); the policy response differs. (Source: Nasdaq/Barchart)
  • Track upcoming high-frequency U.S. inflation and labor signals for confirmation; markets are trading “data dependency” aggressively. (Source: Nasdaq/Barchart)

Oil and gold: commodities reassert macro influence as “inflation risk” returns to the center of the policy debate

Event Synopsis

  • Oil is being treated as the primary transmission channel from geopolitics to the macro outlook, with market discussion centered on the risk of a sharp crude spike if tanker flows face sustained disruption. (Source: The Wall Street Journal)
  • Gold strengthened as a hedge against both geopolitical risk and inflation uncertainty, consistent with a cautious risk backdrop. (Source: AP News)

Cross-Asset Market Reaction

  • Government bonds: U.S. 10Y ~4.00% (little changed); Indonesia 10Y ~6.40% (little changed). (Source: Bank Indonesia)
  • FX: DXY ~97.79 (little changed); USD/IDR ~16,750 (little changed). (Source: Bank Indonesia)
  • Commodities: Brent in the low-$70s area with elevated risk premium; WTI directionally aligned; gold higher on safe-haven demand. (Source: AP News)

Policy Relevance

  • Inflation and expectations management: A persistent oil shock can lift near-term CPI and inflation expectations even if demand weakens, complicating communication and potentially delaying easing cycles. (Source: The Wall Street Journal)
  • External balances and FX: Oil-importing economies face a double hit—worse trade balance and higher inflation—raising the probability of FX depreciation and imported inflation. (Source: AP News)

Forward Watch Points

  • Monitor whether crude strength is “fear premium” only or reflects real supply loss;

Middle East escalation lifts oil risk premium and re-prices inflation risk

Event Synopsis

  • Escalating conflict involving the U.S., Israel, and Iran increased fears of disruption around the Strait of Hormuz, a critical route for global oil flows; market commentary renewed discussion of $90–$100/bbl oil in a disruption scenario.
  • Energy risk dominated the macro narrative over the past day: higher oil is being treated as a near-term inflation shock and a tax on global growth, especially for oil-importing Asia and Europe. (Source: Wall Street Journal)

Cross-Asset Market Reaction

  • Rates: U.S. 10Y near 4.00% (little changed to slightly lower on safe-haven demand); Indonesia 10Y around 6.40% (little changed). (Source: BI)
  • FX: DXY ~97.8 (little changed); USD/IDR ~16,750 (little changed). EUR/USD and USD/JPY were broadly steady versus recent ranges as markets balanced risk-off flows with lower U.S. yields. (Source: BI)
  • Commodities: Brent around the low-$70s and WTI in the high-$60s, both supported by geopolitical risk; gold bid as a hedge. (Source: Reuters)

Policy Relevance

  • Inflation: Higher crude prices can quickly lift headline inflation and inflation expectations, complicating “wait-and-see” stances for central banks even if underlying demand is cooling.
  • Growth and stability: Oil-importing economies face a negative terms-of-trade shock; weaker risk appetite can tighten financial conditions (wider credit spreads, weaker EM FX) even without policy rate changes.

Forward Watch Points

  • Monitor any operational disruption indicators (shipping advisories, insurance premia, tanker diversions) and the persistence of the oil risk premium.
  • Watch whether higher energy prices begin feeding into near-term inflation pricing (breakevens) and prompt more defensive positioning across credit and equities.

China signals discomfort with rapid yuan strength via FX forwards tool change

Event Synopsis

  • China’s central bank announced it will cut the FX risk reserve ratio for forward FX sales to 0% (from 20%), effective March 2, reducing the cost of USD buying via forwards and tempering one-way RMB appreciation pressure.
  • Offshore USD/CNH jumped to about 6.86 immediately after the announcement, indicating policymakers are leaning toward more two-way FX conditions rather than continued rapid RMB gains. (Source: investingLive)

Cross-Asset Market Reaction

  • Rates: U.S. 10Y ~4.00% (little changed); Indonesia 10Y ~6.40% (little changed). (Source: BI)
  • FX: USD/CNH moved higher (RMB weaker); DXY ~97.8 (little changed). USD/IDR ~16,750 (little changed); EUR/USD and USD/JPY largely steady in the last day’s risk mix.
  • Commodities: Oil supported mainly by geopolitics; gold supported by hedging demand; industrial commodities were cautious given uncertainty about China’s growth impulse versus currency management.

Policy Relevance

  • Capital flows and stability: A managed shift toward less RMB strength can help China’s exporters and reduce deflation risk via import prices, but it can also transmit tighter competition to regional peers and influence Asian FX management.
  • Global spillovers: Any renewed RMB weakening bias can export disinflation through traded goods prices, but may also raise EM volatility if interpreted as a growth concern.

Forward Watch Points

  • Track the daily fixing signal and onshore/offshore spread for signs of stronger official resistance to RMB appreciation.
  • Monitor whether Asian FX and local rates re-price toward higher intervention risk (especially in high-beta EM Asia currencies).

Indonesia: rupiah and SBN yields steady, but offshore sovereign funding signals active deficit financing

Event Synopsis

  • Bank Indonesia’s latest stability update showed USD/IDR at 16,750 and the 10Y SBN yield around 6.40%–6.41%, alongside a softer DXY near 97.79 and U.S. 10Y around 4.004% in the referenced window.
  • Separately, Indonesia printed sizeable offshore issuance (Dim Sum and euro tranches), reinforcing that funding is available but highlighting the importance of maintaining investor confidence as fiscal metrics remain closely watched. (Source: BI)

Cross-Asset Market Reaction

  • Rates: U.S. 10Y ~4.00% (little changed); Indonesia 10Y ~6.40% (little changed). (Source: BI)
  • FX: DXY ~97.8 (little changed); USD/IDR ~16,750 (little changed). Regional FX sensitivity remains elevated to oil and China/RMB signals.
  • Commodities: Brent low-$70s and WTI high-$60s supported by geopolitics; gold firm—both relevant for Indonesia via fuel pricing, subsidy/fiscal risks, and trade balance dynamics.

Policy Relevance

  • Inflation and fiscal space: Higher oil risks complicate administered prices/subsidy management; if subsidies rise, fiscal space narrows and can spill into funding needs and risk premia.
  • Capital flows and FX stability: Stable USD/IDR and contained 10Y yields are constructive, but reliance on offshore funding underscores the need to preserve credibility and avoid abrupt policy surprises that could trigger outflows.

Forward Watch Points

  • Watch daily FX liquidity and any signs of renewed pressure on USD/IDR if oil rises further or global risk sentiment deteriorates.
  • Monitor foreign participation in SBN and the pricing/coverage of subsequent government auctions as a real-time read on funding conditions.

Euro area inflation expectations edge down, reinforcing “hold” bias and keeping EUR rates anchored

Event Synopsis

  • The ECB’s Consumer Expectations Survey showed year-ahead inflation expectations eased to 2.6% from 2.8%, and five-year expectations dipped to 2.3% from 2.4%, indicating continued confidence that inflation will stay contained.
  • The data supports the view that the ECB can remain patient, with the main risk being inflation undershooting rather than re-accelerating—unless energy prices reintroduce a sustained shock. (Source: Reuters)

Cross-Asset Market Reaction

  • Rates: U.S. 10Y ~4.00% (little changed); Indonesia 10Y ~6.40% (little changed). European rates were biased lower/steady on softer inflation expectations.
  • FX: DXY ~97.8 (little changed); EUR/USD broadly steady. USD/JPY and USD/CNH were driven more by risk sentiment and China policy than Europe-specific news; USD/IDR ~16,750 (little changed). (Source: BI)
  • Commodities: Oil’s geopolitical premium remained the key swing factor for Europe’s inflation outlook; gold stayed supported as a hedge.

Policy Relevance

  • Inflation outlook: Lower expectations strengthen the case that Europe’s inflation is not entrenched, but a sustained oil shock would most directly challenge this conclusion through headline inflation and second-round effects.
  • Market stability: Anchored expectations reduce the likelihood of abrupt repricing in euro rates; that stabilizes global term premia at the margin, but does not offset energy-driven risk shocks.

Forward Watch Points

  • Track whether energy prices begin lifting euro area near-term inflation expectations (1-year) back up, which would pressure the ECB’s “steady policy” posture.
  • Monitor euro area activity data for any signs that higher energy costs are tightening real incomes and weakening consumption.

Indonesia: Bank Indonesia flagged a firmer rupiah backdrop as yields fell; offshore sovereign issuance underscores funding momentum

Event Synopsis

  • Bank Indonesia’s latest “Rupiah Stability Indicators” update (covering late Feb trading) showed: rupiah closing around IDR 16,750 per USD; Indonesia 10Y government bond yield around 6.41% (and ~6.40% at the next morning open); DXY around 97.79; and US 10Y around 4.004%. (Source: BI)
  • In parallel, Indonesia tapped offshore markets with multi-tranche dim sum (RMB) and euro bonds, extending external funding while markets remain sensitive to fiscal slippage versus the statutory deficit cap. (Source: IFR)

Cross-Asset Market Reaction

  • Government bonds: US 10Y ~4.00% (down on the day in BI snapshot); Indonesia 10Y ~6.40% (down). (Source: BI)
  • Equities: regional risk tone mixed; domestic Indonesia read-through is primarily via rates/FX channel rather than an equity shock in the BI snapshot. (Source: BI)
  • FX: DXY ~97.79 (weaker); USD/IDR ~16,750 (little changed around that level). EUR/USD ~1.18 and USD/JPY ~156 in recent prints. (Source: BI)
  • Commodities: Brent ~US$72–73 and WTI ~US$67; gold remained above ~US$5,100/oz in recent trading. (Source: Yahoo Finance)

Policy Relevance

  • Inflation outlook: a softer DXY and steadier USD/IDR reduce near-term imported inflation risk; higher global oil still poses upside risk to administered fuel prices and expectations. (Source: BI)
  • Growth expectations: lower local yields support domestic financial conditions, but tighter global risk appetite would still transmit via portfolio flows and credit spreads. (Source: BI)
  • Capital flows and FX stability: offshore issuance diversifies funding but can raise near-term FX liquidity management needs; maintaining confidence in the fiscal rule remains key to keeping risk premia contained. (Source: IFR)
  • Policy space: lower yields improve debt-service dynamics at the margin, but oil shocks and global volatility could quickly tighten conditions if FX comes under pressure. (Source: BI)

Forward Watch Points

  • Monitor USD/IDR performance around periods of heavier sovereign/corporate FX funding activity and global risk-off episodes; watch whether local yields decouple from US rates. (Source: BI)
  • Track fiscal signals (deficit trajectory versus the 3% cap) as the main anchor for foreign demand for SBN and the currency risk premium. (Source: IFR)

Oil repriced higher on Middle East risk premium and OPEC+ decision risk, complicating the global inflation path

Event Synopsis

  • Crude moved higher into the March 1 OPEC+ meeting window amid heightened geopolitical uncertainty and focus on whether the group proceeds with a modest monthly supply increase for April. (Source: MEES)
  • Spot levels cited in market reporting around this window: Brent near ~US$72–73/bbl and WTI near ~US$67/bbl, with the move framed as a geopolitical risk premium rather than demand strength. (Source: TradingNEWS)

Cross-Asset Market Reaction

  • Government bonds: US 10Y ~4.00% (little changed versus latest cited levels); Indonesia 10Y ~6.40% (little changed). (Source: BI)
  • Equities: higher oil typically pressures oil-importer equities and supports energy sectors; broader indices have been sensitive to the inflation implications of energy moves. (Source: AP)
  • FX: DXY ~97.7–97.8 range; USD/IDR ~16,750 (little changed). Oil strength generally supports petrocurrencies and can weigh on oil-importers’ external balances. (Source: BI)
  • Commodities: Brent ~US$72–73; WTI ~US$67; gold >US$5,100/oz (supported by risk hedging). (Source: Yahoo Finance)

Policy Relevance

  • Inflation outlook: higher crude raises headline inflation risk globally and can slow disinflation progress, especially where pass-through to retail fuel prices is faster. (Source: MEES)
  • Growth expectations: oil-importing economies face a negative terms-of-trade shock; higher energy costs can reduce real household income and widen current-account deficits. (Source: MEES)
  • Capital flows and stability: renewed inflation fears can keep real yields elevated and tighten global financial conditions, raising refinancing and rollover risks for higher-beta EMs. (Source: MEES)

Forward Watch Points

  • OPEC+ outcome and compliance messaging (especially around any resumption of incremental hikes) and whether prices hold the risk premium or mean-revert quickly. (Source: MEES)
  • Second-round inflation effects: watch inflation expectations and wage-sensitive services inflation, not just the headline print. (Source: Reuters)

Europe: inflation expectations eased again in the ECB consumer survey, reinforcing a “low inflation” baseline

Event Synopsis

  • The ECB’s Consumer Expectations Survey showed median 12-month-ahead inflation expectations fell to 2.6% (from 2.8%), with five-year expectations down to 2.3% (from 2.4%); income growth expectations edged up modestly. (Source: ECB)
  • The signal is consistent with an environment where the main risk is inflation undershooting rather than overheating, especially if energy costs stabilize or fall. (Source: Reuters)

Cross-Asset Market Reaction

  • Government bonds: US 10Y ~4.00%; Indonesia 10Y ~6.40% (both little changed at latest cited levels). (Source: BI)
  • Equities: softer inflation expectations can support rate-sensitive sectors, but the net effect depends on whether markets read it as “benign disinflation” or “weak demand.” (Source: Reuters)
  • FX: EUR/USD around ~1.18 in recent prints; DXY ~97.7–97.8; USD/IDR ~16,750 (little changed). (Source: ECB)
  • Commodities: oil ~US$72–73 Brent; gold >US$5,100/oz (risk hedge and rate expectations channel). (Source: Yahoo Finance)

Policy Relevance

  • Inflation outlook: easing household expectations strengthens the case that euro area inflation psychology is anchored near target, reducing urgency for restrictive policy. (Source: ECB)
  • Growth expectations: if lower inflation expectations reflect weak demand rather than supply-side disinflation, it can imply softer consumption and investment momentum. (Source: Reuters)
  • Capital flows and FX: a more dovish euro area rate path can cap EUR support and keep policy divergence in focus, affecting global portfolio allocation. (Source: Reuters)

Forward Watch Points

  • Watch whether market-based inflation compensation and wage growth corroborate the survey signal, or diverge (a key cross-check for policy credibility). (Source: ECB)
  • Monitor energy-price pass-through: oil volatility can quickly dominate near-term headline inflation even if core pressures are muted. (Source: MEES)

US risk tone: tariffs and policy uncertainty kept “risk-off” behavior in play, supporting bonds and safe havens

Event Synopsis

  • Markets have remained highly sensitive to US trade policy headlines and legal/political uncertainty around tariffs; recent sessions saw investors rotate defensively, with a bias
